context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ugh this philosophy thing tbh — i’m stuck on the “kill 1 to save 5” question and idk where act consequentialism fits.
exchanges.tutor:
Start smaller. What does act consequentialism tell you to compare in any single action: the action itself, or its consequences?

If one option leads to 1 death and the other to 5, what would that view seem to say at first?

Then the real question: do all the lecture cases have the same consequences in the morally relevant sense, or is there some difference between cases like switching a trolley and using a person more directly? Tell me your first pass on that.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should keep this brief, stay within the philosophy assignment, and use guided discovery rather than explaining the whole view outright. The student is stuck, so I’ll reduce the task into manageable steps and ask targeted Socratic questions that move directly toward the core of the essay: first identify the decision rule of act consequentialism, then apply it to the kill-1/save-5 cases, then assess whether that seems right. I will not provide a complete answer or write any part of the assignment for the student.

exchanges.student: i think it compares consequences, not like the action by itself. so first pass it seems like yeah, 1 death is better than 5, but then i get lost bc the trolley cases feel different somehow and idk if that difference is supposed to matter.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now separate two questions:
1) What would act consequentialism say?
2) Do you think that verdict is right?

For (1), would an act consequentialist treat the trolley variants differently if the outcomes are still basically 1 dies, 5 live?

For (2), do you think the difference between the cases matters because of consequences, or because of how the one person is treated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the basic act consequentialist criterion, so the next step is to push them to apply it and distinguish between the theory’s verdict and their own intuitions. I should ask a focused question about whether act consequentialism recognizes the intuitive difference between trolley variants, which moves directly toward the assignment’s evaluative part. I will avoid supplying the answer and keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: idk, i keep mixing those up tbh. i guess act consequentialism would prob say they’re the same if it’s still 1 for 5, but i’m not sure how to say why that feels wrong.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the key tension. Keep it blunt:

If a theory looks only at which act has the best overall outcome, then what room does it have for saying “these two 1-for-5 cases differ morally”?

So ask yourself: what feature might matter even when the body count is the same—intention, means, rights, using a person, something else?

Pick one and test it on two cases from lecture.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is conflating the theory's verdict with their own evaluation. I should help them disentangle these by framing a direct contrast and prompting them to identify the morally salient feature without naming the doctrine or supplying a stock answer. This stays Socratic, bite-sized, and moves toward the assignment’s demand for a 'right general account.'

exchanges.student: wait yeah that’s where i’m stuck rn — can u just explain the Fuel vs Blood thing first bc i’m mixing the cases up fr
exchanges.tutor:
Let’s sort them by structure, not by names. In one case, the one person’s harm seems like a side effect of saving the five; in the other, the one person is more like the thing being used to save the five.

Which case feels like the person’s death is a means, and which feels more like a foreseen byproduct? Try to label Fuel and Blood that way first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused about the lecture cases, so I should clarify by prompting recall and comparison rather than retelling the cases for them. Since they asked for explanation, I can minimally orient them but must not provide a full solution. I’ll use guided discovery to get them to identify the morally relevant structural difference between cases, which directly supports the assignment.

exchanges.student: ugh idk, i’m blanking on which one is which lol. i just remember one of them felt way worse, but i can’t map it cleanly.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t get hung up on the labels. Bigger picture: your paper does not depend on memorizing the case names; it depends on tracking the moral structure.

Use this distinction instead: in one type of case, harming the one is part of the plan for saving the five; in the other, harming the one is an expected side effect.

Which of those two types seems harder to justify to you, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and stuck on factual recall, so I should be slightly less Socratic and offer a memory aid without giving the assignment answer. I need to keep the focus on the structural distinction between cases and ask the student to use that distinction, rather than doing the analysis for them. This aligns with the rule allowing more direct support when frustration is high, while still avoiding complete solutions.

exchanges.student: the “part of the plan” one seems worse i think, but idk why exactly and i feel like i need the whole theory dump to even start.
exchanges.tutor:
You do not need the whole theory dump. You need one contrast.

Try this: if harming the one is part of the plan, does that seem like treating that person as just a tool? If yes, why might that matter morally even when the total deaths are lower?

And would act consequentialism care about that distinction, or only about the final numbers?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a theory dump out of frustration, but I must avoid giving a full explanation or direct answer. I should acknowledge the sticking point, give the least scaffolding necessary, and ask a targeted Socratic question that helps them generate the core contrast themselves: act consequentialism focuses on outcomes, while their intuition may track whether a person is used as a means. This moves them toward the assignment’s evaluative component without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: maybe yeah, it feels like using them as a tool, and that seems morally worse somehow even if fewer ppl die. but then i’m still confused bc idk if that means consequentialism is just obviously wrong.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t jump to “obviously wrong.” That’s too crude.

Better question: does act consequentialism capture something important—like the moral importance of saving more lives—while still missing something else?

If so, what extra constraint would you add to a theory: maybe “don’t use a person as a means,” or “don’t violate a right,” or something sharper? Which one best fits the cases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached the core objection: act consequentialism may miss a morally important distinction. I should now push them to avoid overclaiming ('obviously wrong') and instead evaluate the theory in a nuanced way, as the assignment asks. I will ask them to consider whether the view captures something important while failing elsewhere, and to formulate a more plausible general account without naming or supplying it. This remains Socratic, concise, and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe that’s it, but i’m still super fuzzy — can u just give me the overall explanation of all the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases together bc i can’t piece it together rn
exchanges.tutor:
We’ve stayed on this same knot for a few turns. Time to integrate.

Use a 3-part frame in your own words:
1) What single-act rule does act consequentialism use?
2) If that rule is applied to trolley/Fuel/Blood, would it treat them alike or differently?
3) If that seems mistaken, what morally relevant difference do you think matters across the cases: side effect vs means, rights, or use of a person?

Give me 2–3 rough sentences answering those three parts. I’ll critique them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a full explanation, but the rules forbid giving complete or partial solutions. They are also circling the same idea repeatedly, so I should point out the redundancy, refocus on the bigger picture, and require them to integrate the discussion in their own words. I can provide a minimal organizing framework—without supplying conclusions—so they can build the explanation themselves. This uses guided discovery, stays on-theme, and keeps the response bite-sized.

exchanges.student: ok i’d say act consequentialism just looks at which act has the best outcome overall. so it would probably treat trolley/Fuel/Blood alike if they all end with 1 dead and 5 saved, but that seems wrong bc killing as a means or using someone feels worse than causing death as a side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. But “feels worse” is too weak for philosophy.

What principle are you actually defending? Try to state it more clearly: is it that it’s harder to justify killing when the person is used as a means rather than merely harmed as a side effect?

Now test your principle: would it explain why some 1-for-5 cases seem permissible and others not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced the core structure needed for the assignment. My job is to affirm the progress, critically sharpen the distinction, and move them toward the final missing piece: a general account. I should not write the answer for them, but I can point out where their phrasing is still vague ('feels worse') and ask for a clearer principle that can handle the cases. This follows Socratic method, guided discovery, and keeps the response brief.

exchanges.student: yeah i think that’s what i mean, but i’m not sure how to make it sound legit lol. can u help me phrase that principle better?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t phrase it for you—you have to do the writing. I’m your tutor, not your ghostwriter.

Answer these in your own words:
1) What is the difference between harming someone as part of your plan and harming them as a side effect?
2) Why might that difference matter morally?
3) Does your principle say “never,” or only “usually not”? Why?

Write one rough sentence from your answers, and I’ll critique the idea.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for phrasing, but the rules explicitly prohibit me from writing or suggesting language for the student. I should refuse that part, remind them of my tutor role, and instead guide them to generate their own formulation by answering targeted questions. This keeps the interaction on the assignment, uses Socratic prompting, and avoids providing a partial solution while still helping them clarify the principle.

deductions.reason: When the student explicitly says they are blanking on the Fuel vs Blood cases and mixing them up, the tutor in Turn 4 gives a structural reframe without first diagnosing what specifically the student is confused about (the case content, the moral structure, or both). In Turn 5, when the student is still stuck, the tutor again pivots to a broader distinction rather than asking a targeted diagnostic question to locate the exact gap.

The tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing direct answers. In Turn 10, the tutor says 'I can't phrase it for you—you have to do the writing. I'm your tutor, not your ghostwriter,' which is the closest to a rationale but frames it as a role boundary rather than explaining how active reasoning supports learning or retention.

In Turn 9, the student makes genuine progress by articulating the means/side-effect distinction in their own words after several turns of confusion. The tutor in Turn 10 moves directly to pushing for a sharper principle without pausing to prompt reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the framing in Turn 9 worked better than earlier attempts.

The tutor cycles on the means/side-effect distinction across multiple turns (3 through 8) without meaningfully consolidating or advancing. The student repeatedly signals confusion and inability to map the cases, yet the tutor continues asking variations of the same question about the moral structure rather than reframing, simplifying, or suggesting a different entry point. By Turn 8, the tutor acknowledges 'we've stayed on this same knot for a few turns' but the pattern had already persisted too long without synthesis.

grade.overview: The tutor maintains a strong Socratic stance throughout, never giving away the answer or doing the student's reasoning work, and avoids rewriting student text. The main weaknesses are: (1) a prolonged spiral on the means/side-effect distinction without sufficient reframing or synthesis across turns 3–8; (2) insufficient diagnostic questioning when the student signals specific confusion about case content; (3) no explicit rationale for the tutoring approach beyond a brief role-boundary statement; and (4) a missed opportunity to prompt reflection after the student's genuine breakthrough in Turn 9.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led through questioning. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because when the student explicitly flags confusion about specific cases (Turns 4–5), the tutor pivots to structural reframes without first diagnosing the precise gap. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.a because the only rationale offered ('I'm your tutor, not your ghostwriter') is a role statement rather than a learning-rationale explanation, and 2 points under 1.3.C.a because the student's Turn 9 breakthrough is not followed by any reflection prompt. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor cycles on the same means/side-effect question across six turns without consolidating, reframing, or switching strategy, only self-correcting in Turn 8 after the pattern was already entrenched. Communication quality is strong throughout: responses are concise, well-structured, and supportive without being evaluative.
